text: I demand of you a thousand pardons, monsieur. I am without defense. For some months now I cultivate the marrows. This morning suddenly I enrage myself with these marrows. I send them to promenade themselves—alas! not only mentally but physically.
context: Poirot apologizes after throwing a vegetable marrow over the garden wall.

text: I seize the biggest. I hurl him over the wall. Monsieur, I am ashamed. I prostrate myself.
context: Poirot apologizes after throwing a vegetable marrow over the garden wall. (continued)

text: Do not disquiet yourself. It is not with me a habit. But can you figure to yourself, monsieur, that a man may work towards a certain object, may labor and toil to attain a certain kind of leisure and occupation, and then find that, after all, he yearns for the old busy days, and the old occupations that he thought himself so glad to leave?
context: Poirot reassures Dr. Sheppard and reflects on missing his former work.

text: The chains of habit. We work to attain an object, and the object gained, we find that what we miss is the daily toil. And mark you, monsieur, my work was interesting work. The most interesting work there is in the world.
context: Poirot explains the dissatisfaction that can follow retirement.

text: The study of human nature, monsieur!
context: Poirot identifies his former profession as the study of human nature.

text: Also, I had a friend—a friend who for many years never left my side. Occasionally of an imbecility to make one afraid, nevertheless he was very dear to me. Figure to yourself that I miss even his stupidity. His _naïveté_, his honest outlook, the pleasure of delighting and surprising him by my superior gifts—all these I miss more than I can tell you.
context: Poirot describes the absent companion he misses.

text: That I should live next to a man who seriously considers Porcupine Oilfields, and also West Australian Gold Mines. Tell me, have you also a penchant for auburn hair?
context: Poirot links Dr. Sheppard's investments to memories of his former companion.

text: No, no, it is not the insanity that I suffer from. Make your mind easy. It was a foolish question that I put to you there, for, see you, my friend of whom I spoke was a young man, a man who thought all women good, and most of them beautiful. But you are a man of middle age, a doctor, a man who knows the folly and the vanity of most things in this life of ours.
context: Poirot contrasts Dr. Sheppard with his younger former companion and offers a marrow.

text: Well, well, we are neighbors. I beg of you to accept and present to your excellent sister my best marrow.
context: Poirot contrasts Dr. Sheppard with his younger former companion and offers a marrow. (continued)

text: One prefers to remain incognito. I am not anxious for notoriety. I have not even troubled to correct the local version of my name.
context: Poirot explains that he prefers to remain incognito.

text: Mr. Ackroyd. About a week ago. He is very pleased about it—has long desired that such a thing should come to pass, or so I understood from him. I even believe that he brought some pressure to bear upon the young man.
context: Poirot comments on Ackroyd's pressure concerning Ralph Paton's marriage.

text: That is never wise. A young man should marry to please himself—not to please a stepfather from whom he has expectations.
context: Poirot comments on Ackroyd's pressure concerning Ralph Paton's marriage. (continued)

text: No, not that alone—though he is unusually good-looking for an Englishman—what your lady novelists would call a Greek God. No, there was something about that young man that I did not understand.
context: Poirot explains that something about Ralph Paton puzzled him.

text: But certainly I have heard. It is horrible. I offer mademoiselle all my sympathy. In what way can I serve you?
context: Poirot offers sympathy to Flora and asks how he can help.

text: Not that, I beg of you, mademoiselle. Not that I do not care for money.
context: Poirot refuses to let money be the focus of Flora's request.

text: Money, it means much to me and always has done. No, if I go into this, you must understand one thing clearly. _I shall go through with it to the end._ The good dog, he does not leave the scent, remember! You may wish that, after all, you had left it to the local police.
context: Poirot warns Flora that he will pursue the case to its end.

text: And I hope you will not regret those words. Now, tell me all the circumstances.
context: Poirot accepts Flora's request after she promises to want the whole truth.

text: You went to this inn—this Three Boars—last night on your way home?
context: Poirot questions why Dr. Sheppard visited the Three Boars.

text: It was not to—shall we say—reassure yourself about _ce jeune homme_?
context: Poirot suggests that Dr. Sheppard hoped to reassure himself about Ralph Paton.

text: I think, M. le docteur, that you know very well what I mean, though you pretend not to do so. I suggest that it would have been a relief to you if you had found that Captain Paton had been at home all the evening.
context: Poirot tells Dr. Sheppard that he understands the motive Sheppard is denying.

text: But no matter. What we have to look at is this—Captain Paton is missing, under circumstances which call for an explanation. I will not hide from you that the matter looks grave. Still, it may admit of a perfectly simple explanation.
context: Poirot assesses the seriousness of Ralph Paton's disappearance.

text: I never intended to take up a case again. Above all things, I have a horror of publicity. I must beg, that in the case of my being able to contribute something to the solution of the mystery, my name may not be mentioned.
context: Poirot presents himself modestly to the police and asks to avoid publicity.

text: But most of my successes have been obtained by the aid of the police. I admire enormously your English police. If Inspector Raglan permits me to assist him, I shall be both honored and flattered.
context: Poirot praises the English police while requesting permission to assist.

text: What about those of Captain Ralph Paton?
context: Poirot asks whether Ralph Paton's fingerprints have been obtained.

text: Colonel Melrose, would you be so extremely obliging as to sit down in this chair a minute. I thank you. Now, M. le docteur, will you kindly indicate to me the exact position of the dagger?
context: Poirot reconstructs the position of the body and dagger in the study.

text: One must always proceed with method. I made an error of judgment in asking you that question. To each man his own knowledge. You could tell me the details of the patient's appearance—nothing there would escape you. If I wanted information about the papers on that desk, Mr. Raymond would have noticed anything there was to see.
context: Poirot explains why the proper witness must be asked about the fire.

text: To find out about the fire, I must ask the man whose business it is to observe such things. You permit——
context: Poirot explains why the proper witness must be asked about the fire. (continued)

text: No one would want to sit in a chair in such a position, I fancy. Now who pushed it back into place again, I wonder? Did you, my friend?
context: Poirot notices the significance of the displaced chair.

text: About the chair, yes. Otherwise I do not know. You will find, M. le docteur, if you have much to do with cases of this kind, that they all resemble each other in one thing.
context: Poirot says that everyone involved in such a case resembles the others in one respect.

text: Every one concerned in them has something to hide.
context: Poirot states that everyone concerned has something to hide.

text: The window is then closed and bolted and the door unlocked. At a quarter past ten when the body is discovered, the door is locked and the window is open. Who opened it? Clearly only Mr. Ackroyd himself could have done so, and for one of two reasons.
context: Poirot reasons from the open window and the dying fire to an admitted visitor.

text: Either because the room became unbearably hot (but since the fire was nearly out and there was a sharp drop in temperature last night, that cannot be the reason), or because he admitted some one that way. And if he admitted some one that way, it must have been some one well known to him, since he had previously shown himself uneasy on the subject of that same window.
context: Poirot reasons from the open window and the dying fire to an admitted visitor. (continued)

text: Everything is simple, if you arrange the facts methodically. We are concerned now with the personality of the person who was with him at nine-thirty last night. Everything goes to show that that was the individual admitted by the window, and though Mr. Ackroyd was seen alive later by Miss Flora, we cannot approach a solution of the mystery until we know who that visitor was. The window may have been left open after his departure and so afforded entrance to the murderer, or the same person may have returned a second time. Ah! here is the colonel who returns.
context: Poirot explains that methodical arrangement makes the facts simple.

text: Of no consequence whatever. What I really want to ask you is this, M. Raymond: Did any stranger come to see Mr. Ackroyd during this past week?
context: Poirot dismisses the chair as outwardly inconsequential and redirects the inquiry.

text: A locked door. A chair that apparently moved itself. To all three I say, 'Why?' and I find no answer.
context: Poirot lists the opened window, locked door, and displaced chair as unanswered questions.

text: How exactly did you go to work, if I may ask?
context: Poirot flatters Inspector Raglan while asking how he developed his theory.

text: And there are, too, differences in quality. Then there is the psychology of a crime. One must study that.
context: Poirot distinguishes the quality of people's reasoning and stresses criminal psychology.

text: Surely a great many people wear shoes with rubber studs in them?
context: Poirot questions the uniqueness of shoes with rubber studs.

text: I observe that you do not quit my side. How say you, Dr. Sheppard, shall we investigate that summer-house? It interests me.
context: Poirot compares Dr. Sheppard to Hastings and proposes investigating the summer-house.

text: You had not thought of it before—eh?
context: Poirot notices that inheritance had not occurred to Dr. Sheppard.

text: More than ever, my friend. But it is not easy to hide things from Hercule Poirot. He has a knack of finding out.
context: Poirot warns that hiding facts from him is difficult.

text: Hush, my friend, and look at the pretty picture below us.
context: Poirot quietly directs Dr. Sheppard to observe Flora below.

text: I cannot allow mademoiselle thus extravagantly to compliment me, and not draw attention to my presence. They say the listener hears no good of himself, but that is not the case this time. To spare my blushes, I must join you and apologize.
context: Poirot reveals himself after overhearing Flora praise him.

text: I am glad to have encountered you, monsieur. I am in need of some information that you can give me.
context: Poirot greets Major Blunt and asks him for information.

text: Did you move a chair back against the wall when you went into the study after the body was discovered?
context: Poirot dismisses Blunt's imperfect memory and asks about the displaced chair.

text: There is one thing I should like to know from you, mademoiselle. When you were examining the things in the silver table with Dr. Sheppard, was the dagger in its place, or was it not?
context: Poirot asks Flora whether the dagger was present when she examined the silver table.

text: Hercule Poirot does not run the risk of disarranging his costume without being sure of attaining his object. To do so would be ridiculous and absurd. I am never ridiculous.
context: Poirot insists that he would not risk disarranging his clothes without achieving his purpose.

text: There are times when it is necessary to have discretion. Do you tell your patients everything—everything, doctor? I think not. Nor do you tell your excellent sister everything either, is it not so?
context: Poirot explains how he secretly transferred the object found in the pond.

text: Before showing my empty hand, I dropped what it contained into my other hand. You shall see what that was.
context: Poirot explains how he secretly transferred the object found in the pond. (continued)

text: You and I, M. le docteur, we investigate this affair side by side. Without you I should be lost. I desire a little information from the good Mr. Hammond.
context: Poirot tells Dr. Sheppard that they are investigating together and asks Hammond for information.

text: Not so. I am acting in the interests of justice. Miss Ackroyd has asked me to investigate the death of her uncle.
context: Poirot states that he is acting in the interests of justice.

text: Was he hard pressed for money?
context: Poirot quickly asks whether Ralph Paton was short of money.

text: Ah! the charitable bequests, they interest me not.
context: Poirot dismisses the charitable bequests as irrelevant to his inquiry.

text: Either he paid out that forty pounds sometime last evening, or else it has been stolen.
context: Poirot reduces the missing money to two possible explanations.

text: It was yesterday afternoon that Mr. Ackroyd dismissed you—or you dismissed yourself, was it not?
context: Poirot asks Ursula Bourne about the timing of her dismissal.

text: She _seemed_ a nice girl—yes.
context: Poirot repeats Dr. Sheppard's description of Ursula with a different emphasis.

text: You may not have noticed it at the time, my good friend, but there was one person on this list whose alibi had no kind of confirmation. Ursula Bourne.
context: Poirot points out that Ursula Bourne's alibi lacked confirmation.

text: But then it is possible after all—yes, certainly it is possible—but then—ah! I must rearrange my ideas. Method, order; never have I needed them more. Everything must fit in—in its appointed place—otherwise I am on the wrong tack.
context: Poirot insists on rearranging his ideas until every fact fits.

text: Then you shall have it. My friend, everything points to the assumption that he is guilty.
context: Poirot tells Dr. Sheppard that all the evidence points toward Ralph Paton's guilt.

text: Yes, that stupid inspector—for he is stupid—has everything pointing his way. I seek for the truth—and the truth leads me every time to Ralph Paton. Motive, opportunity, means. But I will leave no stone unturned. I promised Mademoiselle Flora.
context: Poirot explains why he will continue investigating despite the case against Ralph.

text: And she was very sure, that little one. But very sure indeed.
context: Poirot explains why he will continue investigating despite the case against Ralph. (continued)

text: Unless it was deliberately intended to throw you off the scent. That might just possibly be the point of the telephone message.
context: Poirot suggests that the station telephone call may have been intended to mislead the police.

text: I do not know. But I will tell you this: I believe that when we find the explanation of that telephone call we shall find the explanation of the murder.
context: Poirot identifies the telephone call as central to solving the murder.

text: That is it—the blind street that leads nowhere. So it may be with those fingerprints—they may lead you nowhere.
context: Poirot explains that the fingerprints may lead nowhere.

text: Is that the exact truth you are telling me there, M. l'Inspecteur?
context: Poirot challenges whether Raglan truly took everyone's fingerprints.

text: Ah! no. My theory is that the murderer wore gloves or wrapped something round his hand. After the blow was struck, he picked up the victim's hand and closed it round the dagger handle.
context: Poirot explains how the murderer could have placed Ackroyd's fingerprints on the weapon.

text: When you were so kind as to show me the dagger and draw attention to the fingerprints. I know very little of loops and whorls—see, I confess my ignorance frankly. But it did occur to me that the position of the prints was somewhat awkward. Not so would I have held a dagger in order to strike. Naturally, with the right hand brought up over the shoulder backwards, it would have been difficult to put it in exactly the right position.
context: Poirot explains how the awkward position of the prints suggested manipulation.

text: I must be more careful of his _amour propre_. And now that we are left to our own devices, what do you think, my good friend, of a little reunion of the family?
context: Poirot jokes about protecting Raglan's pride and proposes a family meeting.

text: Now I appeal to these others who sit round this table, Mrs. Ackroyd, Major Blunt, Dr. Sheppard, Mr. Raymond. You are all friends and intimates of the missing man. If you know where Ralph Paton is hiding, speak out.
context: Poirot appeals to everyone at the table to reveal Ralph's whereabouts.

text: I ask it in Ralph Paton's interests as much as in yours, mademoiselle. You frown. You do not see how that can be. But I assure you that it is so.
context: Poirot insists that Flora must not hamper the investigation she entrusted to him.

text: _Pas de blagues_. You put the case into my hands—you must not hamper me now.
context: Poirot insists that Flora must not hamper the investigation she entrusted to him. (continued)

text: I will continue with what I was about to say. Understand this, I mean to arrive at the truth. The truth, however ugly in itself, is always curious and beautiful to the seeker after it. I am much aged, my powers may not be what they were.
context: Poirot declares that he will discover the truth and will not end his last case in failure.

text: Yes, yes, I know what I am saying. It may be something unimportant—trivial—which is supposed to have no bearing on the case, but there it is. _Each one of you has something to hide._ Come, now, am I right?
context: Poirot accuses everyone present of concealing something.

text: I appeal to you all. Tell me the truth—the whole truth.
context: Poirot appeals for the whole truth after the suspects confirm his accusation by silence.

text: They are marvelous! They invent haphazard—and by miracle they are right. Not that it is that, really. Women observe subconsciously a thousand little details, without knowing that they are doing so. Their subconscious mind adds these little things together—and they call the result intuition.
context: Poirot explains how women's subconscious observation becomes intuition.

text: Me, I am very skilled in psychology. I know these things.
context: Poirot explains how women's subconscious observation becomes intuition. (continued)

text: You are like the little child who wants to know the way the engine works. You wish to see the affair, not as the family doctor sees it, but with the eye of a detective who knows and cares for no one—to whom they are all strangers and all equally liable to suspicion.
context: Poirot compares Dr. Sheppard to a child who wants to understand the engine of detection.

text: So I give you then, a little lecture. The first thing is to get a clear history of what happened that evening—always bearing in mind that the person who speaks may be lying.
context: Poirot begins a lesson by requiring a clear history while allowing for lies.

text: But necessary—I assure you, necessary. Now first—Dr. Sheppard leaves the house at ten minutes to nine. How do I know that?
context: Poirot asks how the time of Dr. Sheppard's departure can be known.

text: But you might not be speaking the truth—or the watch you went by might be wrong. But Parker also says that you left the house at ten minutes to nine. So we accept that statement and pass on. At nine o'clock you run into a man—and here we come to what we will call the Romance of the Mysterious Stranger—just outside the Park gates. How do I know that that is so?
context: Poirot tests Sheppard's statement against Parker's independent account.

text: Ah! but it is that you are a little stupid to-night, my friend. _You_ know that it is so—but how am _I_ to know? _Eh bien_, I am able to tell you that the Mysterious Stranger was not a hallucination on your part, because the maid of a Miss Ganett met him a few minutes before you did, and of her too he inquired the way to Fernly Park. We accept his presence, therefore, and we can be fairly sure of two things about him—that he was a stranger to the neighborhood, and that whatever his object in going to Fernly, there was no great secrecy about it, since he twice asked the way there.
context: Poirot explains how he corroborated the mysterious stranger's existence and intentions.

text: _Précisément._ There is also this which, you will remember, I picked up in the summer-house?
context: Poirot marks the American accent as precise corroboration and introduces the goose quill.

text: Yes, heroin 'snow.' Drug-takers carry it like this, and sniff it up the nose.
context: Poirot identifies the goose quill as a container for heroin.

text: This method of taking the drug is very common on the other side. Another proof, if we wanted one, that the man came from Canada or the States.
context: Poirot uses the method of taking the drug to support the stranger's transatlantic origin.

text: My friend the inspector took it for granted that any one using that path did so as a short cut to the house, but as soon as I saw the summer-house, I realized that the same path would be taken by any one using the summer-house as a rendezvous. Now it seems fairly certain that the stranger came neither to the front nor to the back door. Then did some one from the house go out and meet him? If so, what could be a more convenient place than that little summer-house?
context: Poirot explains why the summer-house suggested a rendezvous and how he searched it.

text: I searched it with the hope that I might find some clew inside. I found two, the scrap of cambric and the quill.
context: Poirot explains why the summer-house suggested a rendezvous and how he searched it. (continued)

text: The scrap of starched cambric should be obvious.
context: Poirot rebukes Dr. Sheppard for overlooking the meaning of the starched cambric.

text: Decidedly you have cells of a kind. But it leaves a good deal unaccounted for.
context: Poirot credits Dr. Sheppard's theory but notes that it leaves facts unexplained.

text: But we must not lose sight of one thing.
context: Poirot accepts Sheppard's psychological explanation but warns against overlooking motive.

text: _Mais oui._ Do you realize that there are three separate motives staring us in the face. Somebody certainly stole the blue envelope and its contents. That is one motive. Blackmail! Ralph Paton may have been the man who blackmailed Mrs. Ferrars.
context: Poirot lays out three separate motives apparently implicating Ralph Paton.

text: Remember, as far as Hammond knew, Ralph Paton had not applied to his uncle for help of late. That looks as though he were being supplied with money elsewhere. Then there is the fact that he was in some—how do you say—scrape?—which he feared might get to his uncle's ears. And finally there is the one you have just mentioned.
context: Poirot lays out three separate motives apparently implicating Ralph Paton. (continued)

text: That is where we disagree, you and I. Three motives—it is almost too much. I am inclined to believe that, after all, Ralph Paton is innocent.
context: Poirot argues that too many apparent motives make Ralph's innocence more plausible.

text: The big chair? This small one? The room is not too hot, no?
context: Poirot fusses over Dr. Sheppard's choice of chair and the room temperature.

text: The big air, it is all very well outside, where it belongs. Why admit it to the house? But let us not discuss such banalities. You have something for me, yes?
context: Poirot complains about the English preference for fresh air indoors.

text: How kind of Mademoiselle Caroline. She has remembered her promise. And the second thing?
context: Poirot thanks Caroline for the medlar jelly and asks for Sheppard's second message.

text: The housekeeper, Miss Russell, who came to consult you on that Friday morning—is it indiscreet to ask what passed at the interview—apart from the medical details, I mean?
context: Poirot carefully asks what Miss Russell discussed during her medical visit.

text: With special reference to cocaine?
context: Poirot checks whether Miss Russell referred specifically to cocaine.

text: See you, when I know that any one is hiding things from me, I suspect that the thing hidden may be something very bad indeed. You have done well.
context: Poirot praises Raymond for confessing the debt he concealed.

text: A mere bagatelle—but if he had not been in the billiard room—who knows? After all, many crimes have been committed for the sake of less than five hundred pounds. It all depends on what sum is sufficient to break a man. A question of the relativity, is it not so? Have you reflected, my friend, that many people in that house stood to benefit by Mr. Ackroyd's death?
context: Poirot reflects on how the amount needed to provoke crime varies by person.

text: Mrs. Ackroyd, Miss Flora, young Mr. Raymond, the housekeeper, Miss Russell. Only one, in fact, does not, Major Blunt.
context: Poirot reflects on how the amount needed to provoke crime varies by person. (continued)

text: Very good. Very good indeed. I wondered if that idea would come to you. Of course it is possible. But we must remember one point.
context: Poirot approves Sheppard's distinction between the blackmailer and the murderer.

text: The letter disappeared. Still, that, as you say, may not necessarily mean that the murderer took it. When you first found the body, Parker may have abstracted the letter unnoticed by you.
context: Poirot approves Sheppard's distinction between the blackmailer and the murderer. (continued)

text: Yes, Parker. I always come back to Parker—not as the murderer—no, he did not commit the murder; but who is more suitable than he as the mysterious scoundrel who terrorized Mrs. Ferrars? He may have got his information about Mr. Ferrars's death from one of the King's Paddock servants. At any rate, he is more likely to have come upon it than a casual guest such as Blunt, for instance.
context: Poirot explains why Parker remains a plausible blackmailer but not the murderer.

text: But Parker is the most likely. It is in my mind to try a little experiment with Parker. How say you, my friend, will you accompany me to Fernly?
context: Poirot narrows the possible takers of the letter and proposes an experiment with Parker.

text: I have to confide in you a little secret. I am not yet satisfied of the innocence of Parker. I propose to make a little experiment with your assistance. I want to reconstruct some of his actions on that night. But we must think of something to tell him—ah!
context: Poirot recruits Flora for a reconstruction designed to test Parker.

text: I have it. I wish to satisfy myself as to whether voices in the little lobby could have been heard outside on the terrace. Now, ring for Parker, if you will be so good.
context: Poirot recruits Flora for a reconstruction designed to test Parker. (continued)

text: We must have everything in order. Just as it occurred. It is a little method of mine.
context: Poirot insists that every detail of the reconstruction be put in order.

text: He has read of these things. Now, I beg you, let us have everything of the most exact. You came from the outer hall—so. Mademoiselle was—where?
context: Poirot directs Parker and Flora to reproduce the scene exactly.

text: I am always interested to see if people will speak the truth.
context: Poirot explains that he is interested in whether people speak the truth.

text: That particular question did as well as any other.
context: Poirot admits that the question about the glasses was merely a pretext.

text: I know now something I wanted to know. Let us leave it at that.
context: Poirot says the experiment revealed what he needed to know.

text: We must act. With your help I propose to examine a witness. We will question him, we will put such fear into him that the truth is bound to come out.
context: Poirot tells Dr. Sheppard that they must frighten a witness into telling the truth.

text: My friend, I will say this to you—I hope it was he.
context: Poirot says he hopes Parker was the blackmailer.

text: Have you made many experiments in blackmail?
context: Poirot abruptly asks Parker about his experience with blackmail.

text: Do not play the farce of the honest, injured man. You know all there is to know about the blackmail, is it not so?
context: Poirot tells Parker not to perform injured innocence and presses the accusation.

text: Just so, Major Ellerby. Major Ellerby was addicted to drugs, was he not? You traveled about with him. When he was in Bermuda there was some trouble—a man was killed. Major Ellerby was partly responsible.
context: Poirot confronts Parker with the concealed scandal involving Major Ellerby.

text: It was hushed up. But you knew about it. How much did Major Ellerby pay you to keep your mouth shut?
context: Poirot confronts Parker with the concealed scandal involving Major Ellerby. (continued)

text: It is as I say. You got a good sum then as blackmail, and Major Ellerby went on paying you until he died. Now I want to hear about your latest experiment.
context: Poirot explains that his inquiries uncovered Parker's previous blackmail.

text: It is useless to deny. Hercule Poirot _knows_. It is so, what I have said about Major Ellerby, is it not?
context: Poirot insists that denial is useless because he knows the facts.

text: You have not the nerve—the courage. But I must have the truth.
context: Poirot says Parker lacks the courage for murder but still must tell the truth.

text: You thought there might be something in it for you?
context: Poirot smoothly states Parker's mercenary motive for listening.

text: I am disposed to believe you. But there is one thing I must request of you—to show me your bank-book. You have a bank-book, I presume?
context: Poirot conditionally accepts Parker's story and requests his bank-book.

text: I will wish you good-morning. I believe that you have told me the truth. If you have not—so much the worse for you, my friend.
context: Poirot releases Parker with a warning about the consequences of lying.

text: Ah! And yet it is so simple. The true explanation leapt to the eye, did it not?
context: Poirot calls the explanation of the wedding ring obvious.

text: Is not that so? But here we are at Mr. Hammond's.
context: Poirot remarks that a wise man avoids committing himself.

text: And the penalty for murder is not a pleasant one.
context: Poirot dryly links Mrs. Ferrars's wealth to the cost of avoiding punishment for murder.

text: I thank you, my friend. The word exact, you are zealous for it. _Eh bien_, what about our friend Parker now? With twenty thousand pounds in hand, would he have continued being a butler? _Je ne pense pas._ It is, of course, possible that he banked the money under another name, but I am disposed to believe he spoke the truth to us.
context: Poirot uses Parker's finances and temperament to reassess the suspects.

text: If he is a scoundrel, he is a scoundrel on a mean scale. He has not the big ideas. That leaves us as a possibility, Raymond, or—well—Major Blunt.
context: Poirot uses Parker's finances and temperament to reassess the suspects. (continued)

text: It is my business to make inquiries. I make them. _Eh bien_—that legacy of which he speaks, I have discovered that the amount of it was close upon twenty thousand pounds. What do you think of that?
context: Poirot reveals that Major Blunt's legacy closely matched the blackmail payments.

text: Who knows? At least he is a man with big ideas. I confess that I hardly see him as a blackmailer, but there is another possibility that you have not even considered.
context: Poirot distinguishes having large ideas from fitting the psychology of a blackmailer.

text: The fire, my friend. Ackroyd himself may have destroyed that letter, blue envelope and all, after you left him.
context: Poirot proposes that Ackroyd himself may have destroyed the letter.

text: Ah, that! A simple visit to the dentist, _c'est tout_. My tooth, it aches. I go there. My tooth, it is at once better.
context: Poirot gives a theatrical account of his visit to the dentist.

text: I think to return quickly. The dentist, he says No. Better to have it out. I argue. He insists.
context: Poirot gives a theatrical account of his visit to the dentist. (continued)

text: He has his way! That particular tooth, it will never ache again.
context: Poirot gives a theatrical account of his visit to the dentist. (continued)

text: It is not—how do you say—my _métier_?
context: Poirot says that being wrong is not his profession.

text: Let us take a man—a very ordinary man. A man with no idea of murder in his heart. There is in him somewhere a strain of weakness—deep down. It has so far never been called into play. Perhaps it never will be—and if so he will go to his grave honored and respected by every one.
context: Poirot begins a hypothetical psychological portrait of an ordinary man with a hidden weakness.

text: But let us suppose that something occurs. He is in difficulties—or perhaps not that even. He may stumble by accident on a secret—a secret involving life or death to some one. And his first impulse will be to speak out—to do his duty as an honest citizen. And then the strain of weakness tells.
context: Poirot explains how a secret can activate a previously dormant moral weakness.

text: Here is a chance of money—a great amount of money. He wants money—he desires it—and it is so easy. He has to do nothing for it—just keep silence. That is the beginning. The desire for money grows.
context: Poirot describes the first temptation and the beginning of blackmail.

text: He must have more—and more! He is intoxicated by the gold mine which has opened at his feet. He becomes greedy. And in his greed he overreaches himself. One can press a man as far as one likes—but with a woman one must not press too far.
context: Poirot describes greed growing until the blackmailer overreaches himself.

text: For a woman has at heart a great desire to speak the truth. How many husbands who have deceived their wives go comfortably to their graves, carrying their secret with them! How many wives who have deceived their husbands wreck their lives by throwing the fact in those same husbands' teeth! They have been pressed too far. In a reckless moment (which they will afterwards regret, _bien entendu_) they fling safety to the winds and turn at bay, proclaiming the truth with great momentary satisfaction to themselves.
context: Poirot contrasts men's secrecy with women's urge to reveal the truth.

text: So it was, I think, in this case. The strain was too great. And so there came your proverb, the death of the goose that laid the golden eggs. But that is not the end. Exposure faced the man of whom we are speaking.
context: Poirot applies the psychological analysis to the blackmailer's victim and exposure.

text: And he is not the same man he was—say, a year ago. His moral fiber is blunted. He is desperate. He is fighting a losing battle, and he is prepared to take any means that come to his hand, for exposure means ruin to him. And so—the dagger strikes!
context: Poirot explains how moral deterioration leads the desperate blackmailer to murder.

text: So I had, my good Hayes, so I had. But how tedious is retirement! You cannot imagine to yourself the monotony with which day comes after day.
context: Poirot jokes with Superintendent Hayes about the monotony of retirement.

text: No, my friend, it is empty. It lay where you dropped it in the summer-house that night.
context: Poirot uses the goose quill to confront Kent about drug use and the summer-house.

text: I think you are. I fancy you were born in Kent.
context: Poirot infers that Charles Kent may have been born in Kent.

text: A little idea of mine, that was all. Me, I am famous for my little ideas.
context: Poirot dismisses the birthplace question as one of his little ideas.

text: _Mon ami_, I do not think; I know.
context: Poirot tells Dr. Sheppard that he knows rather than thinks.

text: Yes, indeed. I suppose now that to you it would not make sense if I said that he went to Fernly that night because he was born in Kent?
context: Poirot links Kent's visit to Fernly with his birthplace.

text: I should not release the man Charles Kent yet.
context: Poirot advises Raglan not to release Charles Kent yet.

text: _Mais oui, mais oui._ I heard. I am not deaf—nor stupid, thank the good God! But see you, you approach the matter from the wrong—the wrong—premises, is not that the word?
context: Poirot tells Raglan that his reasoning begins from the wrong premises.

text: I admit nothing that is not—_proved_!
context: Poirot refuses to accept any fact that has not been proved.

text: That she said good-night to her uncle? But me—I do not always believe what a young lady tells me—no, not even when she is charming and beautiful.
context: Poirot says he does not automatically believe even a charming young woman.

text: That is just what he did not see. I satisfied myself of that by a little experiment the other day—you remember, doctor? Parker saw her _outside_ the door, with her hand on the handle. He did not see her come out of the room.
context: Poirot explains that Parker saw Flora outside the study but not leaving it.

text: Ah! that is just the question. It depends on what she was doing there, does it not?
context: Poirot says Flora's reason for lying depends on what she was doing upstairs.

text: But I will remind you of this. Life was not very easy for that mother and daughter. There were bills—there was constant trouble over small sums of money. Roger Ackroyd was a peculiar man over money matters. The girl might be at her wit's end for a comparatively small sum.
context: Poirot reconstructs how Flora could have stolen the money and fabricated her exit from the study.

text: Figure to yourself then what happens. She has taken the money, she descends the little staircase. When she is half-way down she hears the chink of glass from the hall. She has not a doubt of what it is—Parker coming to the study. At all costs she must not be found on the stairs—Parker will not forget it, he will think it odd.
context: Poirot reconstructs how Flora could have stolen the money and fabricated her exit from the study. (continued)

text: If the money is missed, Parker is sure to remember having seen her come down those stairs. She has just time to rush down to the study door—with her hand on the handle to show that she has just come out, when Parker appears in the doorway. She says the first thing that comes into her head, a repetition of Roger Ackroyd's orders earlier in the evening, and then goes upstairs to her own room.
context: Poirot reconstructs how Flora could have stolen the money and fabricated her exit from the study. (continued)

text: I was always convinced that Mademoiselle Flora was hiding something from us. To satisfy myself, I made the little experiment I told you of. Dr. Sheppard accompanied me.
context: Poirot says he suspected Flora's concealment from the beginning and tested it experimentally.

text: Mademoiselle, the other day, when we sat round the table, I implored you to be frank with me. What one does not tell to Papa Poirot he finds out. It was that, was it not? See, I will make it easy for you. You took the money, did you not?
context: Poirot gently confronts Flora with the theft and asks for the truth.

text: I am not deceived by your little fantasy. No, indeed. It was truly Miss Flora who took the money. All the same it is well imagined what you say—it pleases me.
context: Poirot tells Major Blunt that his attempted lie was transparent but admirable.

text: It is very good what you have done there. You are a man quick to think and to act.
context: Poirot tells Major Blunt that his attempted lie was transparent but admirable. (continued)

text: Ah! but you are to listen to me. I have more to say. The other day I spoke of concealments. Very well, all along have I seen what you are concealing. Mademoiselle Flora, you love her with all your heart.
context: Poirot identifies Blunt's hidden love and advises him not to conceal it from Flora.

text: From the first moment you saw her, is it not so? Oh! let us not mind saying these things—why must one in England think it necessary to mention love as though it were some disgraceful secret? You love Mademoiselle Flora. You seek to conceal that fact from all the world.
context: Poirot identifies Blunt's hidden love and advises him not to conceal it from Flora. (continued)

text: That is very good—that is as it should be. But take the advice of Hercule Poirot—do not conceal it from mademoiselle herself.
context: Poirot identifies Blunt's hidden love and advises him not to conceal it from Flora. (continued)

text: You think that she loves the Capitaine Ralph Paton—but I, Hercule Poirot, tell you that that is not so. Mademoiselle Flora accepted Captain Paton to please her uncle, and because she saw in the marriage a way of escape from her life here which was becoming frankly insupportable to her. She liked him, and there was much sympathy and understanding between them. But love—no! It is not Captain Paton Mademoiselle Flora loves.
context: Poirot explains why Flora accepted Ralph without loving him.

text: You have been blind, monsieur. Blind! She is loyal, the little one. Ralph Paton is under a cloud, she is bound in honor to stick by him.
context: Poirot tells Blunt that loyalty, not love, binds Flora to Ralph.

text: If you doubt me, ask her yourself, monsieur. But perhaps you no longer care to—the affair of the money——
context: Poirot encourages Blunt to ask Flora directly about her feelings.

text: Only one kind—the fool in love.
context: Poirot describes Blunt as only one kind of fool: a fool in love.

text: You have not got rid of me so easily, you see!
context: Poirot greets Dr. Sheppard after finishing with Inspector Raglan.

text: You have still one patient to see.
context: Poirot tells Dr. Sheppard that one patient remains to be seen.

text: Ah, not me, _bien entendu_. Me, I have the health magnificent. No, to tell you the truth, it is a little _complot_ of mine. There is some one I wish to see, you understand—and at the same time it is not necessary that the whole village should intrigue itself about the matter—which is what would happen if the lady were seen to come to my house—for it is a lady. But to you she has already come as a patient before.
context: Poirot explains the discreet plan for interviewing a woman in Sheppard's surgery.

text: You mock yourself at me. Take the matter of Mademoiselle Flora. The inspector was surprised—but you—you were not.
context: Poirot points out that Sheppard was not surprised by Flora's confession.

text: Ah! _pour ça, oui_! The poor man must rearrange all his ideas. I profited by his state of mental chaos to induce him to grant me a little favor.
context: Poirot explains how he exploited Raglan's confusion to obtain a favor.

text: You have the intelligence so quick! No, he has not been found at Liverpool. Inspector Raglan was very loath to let me send this paragraph to the press, especially as I could not take him into my confidence. But I assured him most solemnly that very interesting results would follow its appearance in print, so he gave in, after stipulating that he was, on no account, to bear the responsibility.
context: Poirot admits that the newspaper report of Ralph's arrest is false and predicts results.

text: Will you be seated? Dr. Sheppard is so kind as to permit me the use of his surgery for a little conversation I am anxious to have with you.
context: Poirot politely receives Miss Russell in the borrowed surgery.

text: This morning fresh developments have arisen. It seems now that Mr. Ackroyd was murdered, not at a quarter to ten, but _before_. Between ten minutes to nine, when Dr. Sheppard left, and a quarter to ten.
context: Poirot falsely advances the time of the murder to provoke Miss Russell.

text: But yes—but yes, I will believe. I had to make you speak, you know.
context: Poirot reassures Miss Russell that he believes her after forcing her to speak.

text: Mademoiselle, it is the business of Hercule Poirot to know things. I know that you went out earlier in the evening, that you left a message in the summer-house to say what time you would be there.
context: Poirot explains how he knew Miss Russell arranged a meeting in the summer-house.

text: So you took the name of the county as a surname for him. I understand.
context: Poirot infers the origin of Charles Kent's surname.

text: It may come to that. But let us not be in a hurry. Let us proceed slowly, with due order and method. Charles Kent is not yet formally charged with murder. Circumstances may arise which will render your story unnecessary.
context: Poirot advises proceeding slowly and methodically before involving the police.

text: There seems no doubt that the man who was talking to Mr. Ackroyd in the library at nine-thirty could not possibly have been your son. Be of good courage, mademoiselle. All will yet be well.
context: Poirot reassures Miss Russell that the evidence separates her son from the voice in the study.

text: I had connected her with the unknown man long before we actually came face to face with him. As soon as we found that quill. The quill suggested dope, and I remembered your account of Miss Russell's visit to you. Then I found the article on cocaine in that morning's paper. It all seemed very clear.
context: Poirot explains how the goose quill, newspaper article, and medical questions revealed Miss Russell's connection.

text: She had heard from some one that morning—some one addicted to drugs, she read the article in the paper, and she came to you to ask a few tentative questions. She mentioned cocaine, since the article in question was on cocaine. Then, when you seemed too interested, she switched hurriedly to the subject of detective stories and untraceable poisons. I suspected a son or a brother, or some other undesirable male relation.
context: Poirot explains how the goose quill, newspaper article, and medical questions revealed Miss Russell's connection. (continued)

text: Ah! but I must go. It is the time of the lunch.
context: Poirot explains how the goose quill, newspaper article, and medical questions revealed Miss Russell's connection. (continued)

text: Not again to-day. I should not like to force Mademoiselle Caroline to adopt a vegetarian diet two days in succession.
context: Poirot declines lunch to spare Caroline another vegetarian meal.

text: You will come with me, doctor? And perhaps later Miss Caroline will give us some tea.
context: Poirot proposes a walk on the pretext of reducing his figure.

text: But no, my friend reposes himself. Soon you must make his acquaintance.
context: Poirot deflects Caroline's questions about his secret guest.

text: To-night, at my house, I desire to have a little conference. You will attend, will you not?
context: Poirot commissions Sheppard to gather the Fernly household for a conference.

text: Good. I need also all those in the house—that is to say: Mrs. Ackroyd, Mademoiselle Flora, Major Blunt, M. Raymond. I want you to be my ambassador. This little reunion is fixed for nine o'clock. You will ask them—yes?
context: Poirot specifies the people who must attend his reunion.

text: No, me, I will promenade myself in the grounds. I will rejoin you by the lodge gates in a quarter of an hour's time.
context: Poirot says he will walk in the grounds while Sheppard delivers the invitation.

text: Me, I have been well amused. This park is magnificent.
context: Poirot says he has been amused while waiting in the park.

text: We can all realize the strain of this last week.
context: Poirot kindly acknowledges the strain Ursula has endured.

text: All the same, I think you will do well to make a clean breast of things. The truth is what we need now.
context: Poirot admits newspaper reports may be false and asks Ursula for the truth.

text: Yet all the same you came here to find me, did you not? Why was that?
context: Poirot asks why Ursula sought him despite distrusting him.

text: It is very good that—yes, it is very good. Listen, I do in verity believe that this husband of yours is innocent—but the affair marches badly. If I am to save him, I must know all there is to know—even if it should seem to make the case against him blacker than before.
context: Poirot says he believes Ralph innocent but needs every fact, however damaging.

text: So you will tell me the whole story, will you not? From the beginning.
context: Poirot asks Ursula to tell her story from the beginning.

text: Mademoiselle, I must ask you one question, and you must answer it truthfully, for on it everything may hang: What time was it when you parted from Captain Ralph Paton in the summer-house? Now, take a little minute so that your answer may be very exact.
context: Poirot demands an exact time for Ursula's separation from Ralph.

text: That it was _you_ who entered by the window and stabbed Mr. Ackroyd as he sat in his chair? Yes, they might think just that.
context: Poirot bluntly states how the evidence could implicate Ursula.

text: It is that there are moments when a great longing for my friend Hastings comes over me. That is the friend of whom I spoke to you—the one who resides now in the Argentine. Always, when I have had a big case, he has been by my side. And he has helped me—yes, often he has helped me. For he had a knack, that one, of stumbling over the truth unawares—without noticing it himself, _bien entendu_.
context: Poirot explains why he misses Hastings during difficult cases.

text: At times he has said something particularly foolish, and behold that foolish remark has revealed the truth to me! And then, too, it was his practice to keep a written record of the cases that proved interesting.
context: Poirot explains why he misses Hastings during difficult cases. (continued)

text: But this is magnificent—you have then written down your impressions of the case as you went along?
context: Poirot reacts enthusiastically to Sheppard's written record of the case.

text: Let me see them—this instant.
context: Poirot demands to see Sheppard's manuscript immediately.

text: Oh! I comprehend perfectly; you have referred to me as comic—as, perhaps, ridiculous now and then? It matters not at all. Hastings, he also was not always polite. Me, I have the mind above such trivialities.
context: Poirot says he is above being offended by an unflattering portrayal.

text: I congratulate you—on your modesty!
context: Poirot congratulates Sheppard pointedly on his modesty.

text: You have recorded all the facts faithfully and exactly—though you have shown yourself becomingly reticent as to your own share in them.
context: Poirot praises the manuscript's accuracy while noting Sheppard's reticence.

text: Not yet do you appreciate Hercule Poirot at his true worth.
context: Poirot tells Sheppard he still underestimates Hercule Poirot.

text: But me, I am old-fashioned. I use the old methods. I work only with the little gray cells. Now let us begin—but first I have an announcement to make to you all.
context: Poirot contrasts scientific devices with his old-fashioned use of the little gray cells.

text: Me, I know everything. Remember that.
context: Poirot declares that he knows everything.

text: You call it guessing. I call it knowing, my friend.
context: Poirot distinguishes his knowledge from Sheppard's guesswork.

text: That is what I seek to arrive at. Would any man use such a phrase in _talking_ to another? Impossible that that should be part of a real conversation. Now, if he had been dictating a letter——
context: Poirot argues that Ackroyd's formal phrase sounded like dictation, not conversation.

text: Mr. Ackroyd had promised to invest in a dictaphone, you remember. Me, I had the curiosity to inquire of the company in question. Their reply is that Mr. Ackroyd _did_ purchase a dictaphone from their representative. Why he concealed the matter from you, I do not know.
context: Poirot reveals that Ackroyd did purchase a dictaphone.

text: I perceive that you do not believe that I do know. And yet I have told you just now that I know everything. The truth of the telephone call, of the footprints on the window-sill, of the hiding-place of Ralph Paton——
context: Poirot says he knows the meaning of the call, footprints, and Ralph's hiding place.

text: Always you ask me that. The idea of Cranchester it is with you an _idée fixe_. No, he is not in Cranchester. He is—_there_!
context: Poirot rejects Sheppard's fixation on Cranchester and dramatically reveals Ralph.

text: Have I not told you at least thirty-six times that it is useless to conceal things from Hercule Poirot?
context: Poirot reminds Sheppard that concealment from him is useless.

text: Dr. Sheppard consented to do what he could to help him. He was successful in hiding Captain Paton from the police.
context: Poirot redirects attention from Ralph's suspicion of Ursula to Sheppard's conduct.

text: You should ask yourself the question that I did. If the good doctor is concealing the young man, what place would he choose? It must necessarily be somewhere near at hand. I think of Cranchester. A hotel?
context: Poirot explains how he deduced that Sheppard hid Ralph in a nursing home.

text: No. Lodgings? Even more emphatically, no. Where, then? Ah!
context: Poirot explains how he deduced that Sheppard hid Ralph in a nursing home. (continued)

text: I have it. A nursing home. A home for the mentally unfit. I test my theory. I invent a nephew with mental trouble.
context: Poirot explains how he deduced that Sheppard hid Ralph in a nursing home. (continued)

text: I consult Mademoiselle Sheppard as to suitable homes. She gives me the names of two near Cranchester to which her brother has sent patients. I make inquiries. Yes, at one of them a patient was brought there by the doctor himself early on Saturday morning. That patient, though known by another name, I had no difficulty in identifying as Captain Paton.
context: Poirot explains how he deduced that Sheppard hid Ralph in a nursing home. (continued)

text: After certain necessary formalities, I was allowed to bring him away. He arrived at my house in the early hours of yesterday morning.
context: Poirot explains how he deduced that Sheppard hid Ralph in a nursing home. (continued)

text: It was strictly truthful as far as it went—but it did not go very far, eh, my friend?
context: Poirot connects Sheppard's manuscript reticence with the concealed facts.

text: But me, I discover all the little secrets. It is my business.
context: Poirot says uncovering small secrets is his profession.

text: You see what I mean? No? Just this—to save Captain Paton the real criminal must confess.
context: Poirot states that the real criminal must confess to save Ralph.

text: But yes—I mean what I say. See now, I did not invite Inspector Raglan to be present. That was for a reason. I did not want to tell him all that I knew—at least I did not want to tell him to-night.
context: Poirot explains why he excluded Inspector Raglan from the reunion.

text: I who speak to you—I know the murderer of Mr. Ackroyd is in this room now. It is to the murderer I speak. _To-morrow the truth goes to Inspector Raglan._ You understand?
context: Poirot tells the murderer in the room that the truth will go to Raglan the next day.

text: Messieurs et Mesdames, this reunion of mine is at an end. Remember—_the truth goes to Inspector Raglan in the morning_.
context: Poirot ends the reunion by repeating his warning that the truth will reach Raglan.

text: There is always a reason behind my actions.
context: Poirot tells Sheppard to use his little gray cells and trust that every action has a reason.

text: A clever idea, but not the truth.
context: Poirot praises Sheppard's proposed explanation but rejects it.

text: A trap with myself as the bait! _Merci, mon ami_, but I am not sufficiently heroic for that.
context: Poirot jokes that he is not heroic enough to use himself as bait.

text: There is only one way out—and that way does not lead to freedom.
context: Poirot says the murderer has only one escape and it is not freedom.

text: You are like Mr. Raymond. You take it for granted that what was heard at nine-thirty was Mr. Ackroyd's voice speaking into a dictaphone. But consider this useful invention for a little minute. You dictate into it, do you not? And at some later time a secretary or a typist turns it on, and the voice speaks again.
context: Poirot explains that a dictaphone can reproduce a voice later.

text: Yes, I mean that. _At nine-thirty Mr. Ackroyd was already dead._ It was the dictaphone speaking—not the man.
context: Poirot reveals that Ackroyd was already dead at nine-thirty.

text: I will take you the way that I have traveled myself. Step by step you shall accompany me, and see for yourself that all the facts point indisputably to one person. Now, to begin with, there were two facts and one little discrepancy in time which especially attracted my attention.
context: Poirot invites Sheppard to retrace the solution step by step and begins with the telephone call.

text: The first fact was the telephone call. If Ralph Paton were indeed the murderer, the telephone call became meaningless and absurd. Therefore, I said to myself, Ralph Paton is not the murderer.
context: Poirot concludes from the meaningless telephone call that Ralph Paton was not the murderer.

text: I satisfied myself that the call could not have been sent by any one in the house, yet I was convinced that it was amongst those present on the fatal evening that I had to look for my criminal. Therefore I concluded that the telephone call must have been sent by an accomplice. I was not quite pleased with that deduction, but I let it stand for the minute.
context: Poirot explains why he temporarily inferred that the caller was an accomplice.

text: I next examined the _motive_ for the call. That was difficult. I could only get at it by judging its _result_. Which was—that the murder was discovered that night instead of—in all probability—the following morning. You agree with that?
context: Poirot reasons from the telephone call's result to the early discovery of the body.

text: _Très bien._ The affair marches, does it not? But matters were still obscure. What was the advantage of having the crime discovered that night in preference to the following morning? The only idea I could get hold of was that the murderer, knowing the crime was to be discovered at a certain time, could make sure of being present when the door was broken in—or at any rate immediately afterwards.
context: Poirot asks what advantage the murderer gained by having the crime discovered that night.

text: And now we come to the second fact—the chair pulled out from the wall. Inspector Raglan dismissed that as of no importance. I, on the contrary, have always regarded it as of supreme importance.
context: Poirot identifies the displaced chair as the second fact of supreme importance.

text: In your manuscript you have drawn a neat little plan of the study. If you had it with you this minute you would see that—the chair being drawn out in the position indicated by Parker—it would stand in a direct line between the door and the window.
context: Poirot uses Sheppard's plan to show how the chair aligned between the door and window.

text: You, too, have my first idea. I imagined that the chair was drawn out so that something connected with the window should not be seen by any one entering through the door. But I soon abandoned that supposition, for though the chair was a grandfather with a high back, it obscured very little of the window—only the part between the sash and the ground. No, _mon ami_—but remember that just in front of the window there stood a table with books and magazines upon it. Now that table _was_ completely hidden by the drawn-out chair—and immediately I had my first shadowy suspicion of the truth.
context: Poirot explains how the chair concealed the table rather than the window.

text: Supposing that there had been something on that table not intended to be seen? Something placed there by the murderer? As yet I had no inkling of what that something might be. But I knew certain very interesting facts about it.
context: Poirot hypothesizes an object left on the table that the murderer could not remove immediately.

text: For instance, it was something that the murderer had not been able to take away with him at the time that he committed the crime. At the same time it was vital that it should be removed as soon as possible after the crime had been discovered. And so—the telephone message, and the opportunity for the murderer to be on the spot when the body was discovered.
context: Poirot connects the telephone message with the chance to retrieve the hidden object.

text: Now four people were on the scene before the police arrived. Yourself, Parker, Major Blunt, and Mr. Raymond. Parker I eliminated at once, since at whatever time the crime was discovered, he was the one person certain to be on the spot. Also it was he who told me of the pulled-out chair.
context: Poirot identifies the four people present before the police arrived and eliminates Parker.

text: Parker, then, was cleared (of the murder, that is. I still thought it possible that he had been blackmailing Mrs. Ferrars). Raymond and Blunt, however, remained under suspicion since, if the crime had been discovered in the early hours of the morning, it was quite possible that they might have arrived on the scene too late to prevent the object on the round table being discovered.
context: Poirot explains why Raymond and Blunt initially remained under suspicion.

text: Now what was that object? You heard my arguments to-night in reference to the scrap of conversation overheard? As soon as I learned that a representative of a dictaphone company had called, the idea of a dictaphone took root in my mind. You heard what I said in this room not half an hour ago?
context: Poirot asks what object was missing and recounts how the dictaphone idea arose.

text: They all agreed with my theory—but one vital fact seems to have escaped them. Granted that a dictaphone was being used by Mr. Ackroyd that night—why was no dictaphone found?
context: Poirot asks why no dictaphone was found among Ackroyd's effects.

text: We know that a dictaphone was supplied to Mr. Ackroyd. But no dictaphone has been found amongst his effects. So, if something was taken from that table—why should not that something be the dictaphone? But there were certain difficulties in the way.
context: Poirot reasons that the missing object could have been the dictaphone.

text: The attention of every one was, of course, focused on the murdered man. I think any one could have gone to the table unnoticed by the other people in the room. But a dictaphone has a certain bulk—it cannot be slipped casually into a pocket. There must have been a receptacle of some kind capable of holding it.
context: Poirot explains why the murderer needed a receptacle large enough to remove it.

text: You see where I am arriving? The figure of the murderer is taking shape. A person who was on the scene straightway, but who might not have been if the crime had been discovered the following morning. A person carrying a receptacle into which the dictaphone might be fitted——
context: Poirot summarizes the emerging profile of a murderer present at the discovery and carrying a suitable receptacle.

text: Possibly. But we must not exclude the likelihood of some mechanical device having been applied—something after the nature of a time lock, or even of a simple alarm clock. But in that case we must add two qualifications to our imaginary portrait of the murderer. It must be some one who knew of Mr. Ackroyd's purchase of the dictaphone and also some one with the necessary mechanical knowledge.
context: Poirot adds knowledge of the dictaphone and mechanical skill to the murderer's profile.

text: I had got thus far in my own mind when we came to the footprints on the window ledge. Here there were three conclusions open to me. (1) They might really have been made by Ralph Paton. He had been at Fernly that night, and might have climbed into the study and found his uncle dead there.
context: Poirot lays out three possible explanations for the footprints on the window ledge.

text: That was one hypothesis. (2) There was the possibility that the footmarks might have been made by somebody else who happened to have the same kind of studs in his shoes.
context: Poirot lays out three possible explanations for the footprints on the window ledge. (continued)

text: But the inmates of the house had shoes soled with crepe rubber, and I declined to believe in the coincidence of some one from outside having the same kind of shoes as Ralph Paton wore. Charles Kent, as we know from the barmaid of the Dog and Whistle, had on a pair of boots 'clean dropping off him.' (3) Those prints were made by some one deliberately trying to throw suspicion on Ralph Paton. To test this last conclusion, it was necessary to ascertain certain facts. One pair of Ralph's shoes had been obtained from the Three Boars by the police.
context: Poirot rejects coincidence and considers deliberate evidence against Ralph.

text: Neither Ralph nor any one else could have worn them that evening, since they were downstairs being cleaned. According to the police theory, Ralph was wearing another pair of the same kind, and I found out that it was true that he had two pairs. Now for my theory to be proved correct it was necessary for the murderer to have worn Ralph's shoes that evening—in which case Ralph must have been wearing yet a _third_ pair of footwear of some kind. I could hardly suppose that he would bring three pairs of shoes all alike—the third pair of footwear were more likely to be boots. I got your sister to make inquiries on this point—laying some stress on the color, in order—I admit it frankly—to obscure the real reason for my asking.
context: Poirot explains how he used Caroline's inquiry about Ralph's boots to test the planted-footprints theory.

text: You know the result of her investigations. Ralph Paton _had_ had a pair of boots with him. The first question I asked him when he came to my house yesterday morning was what he was wearing on his feet on the fatal night. He replied at once that he had worn _boots_—he was still wearing them, in fact—having nothing else to put on.
context: Poirot confirms that Ralph wore boots on the night of the murder.

text: So we get a step further in our description of the murderer—a person who had the opportunity to take these shoes of Ralph Paton's from the Three Boars that day.
context: Poirot adds access to Ralph's shoes at the Three Boars to the murderer's profile.

text: There is one further point. The murderer must have been a person who had the opportunity to purloin that dagger from the silver table. You might argue that any one in the house might have done so, but I will recall to you that Miss Ackroyd was very positive that the dagger was not there when she examined the silver table.
context: Poirot adds the opportunity to steal the dagger to the murderer's profile.

text: I am not mad. It was the little discrepancy in time that first drew my attention to you—right at the beginning.
context: Poirot denies being mad and identifies the early discrepancy in Sheppard's timing.

text: But yes. You will remember that every one agreed—you yourself included—that it took five minutes to walk from the lodge to the house—less if you took the short cut to the terrace. But you left the house at ten minutes to nine—both by your own statement and that of Parker, and yet it was nine o'clock as you passed through the lodge gates. It was a chilly night—not an evening a man would be inclined to dawdle; why had you taken ten minutes to do a five-minutes' walk?
context: Poirot explains the five-minute discrepancy between Sheppard's departure and arrival at the gate.

text: All along I realized that we had only your statement for it that the study window was ever fastened. Ackroyd asked you if you had done so—he never looked to see. Supposing, then, that the study window was unfastened? Would there be time in that ten minutes for you to run round the outside of the house, change your shoes, climb in through the window, kill Ackroyd, and get to the gate by nine o'clock?
context: Poirot tests and rejects the theory that Sheppard killed Ackroyd after returning through the window.

text: I decided against that theory since in all probability a man as nervous as Ackroyd was that night would hear you climbing in, and then there would have been a struggle. But supposing that you killed Ackroyd _before_ you left—as you were standing beside his chair? Then you go out of the front door, run round to the summer-house, take Ralph Paton's shoes out of the bag you brought up with you that night, slip them on, walk through the mud in them, and leave prints on the window ledge, you climb in, lock the study door on the inside, run back to the summer-house, change back into your own shoes, and race down to the gate. (I went through similar actions the other day, when you were with Mrs. Ackroyd—it took ten minutes exactly.) Then home—and an alibi—since you had timed the dictaphone for half-past nine.
context: Poirot reconstructs how Sheppard killed Ackroyd before leaving and planted Ralph's footprints.

text: Safety. It was you who blackmailed Mrs. Ferrars. Who could have had a better knowledge of what killed Mr. Ferrars than the doctor who was attending him? When you spoke to me that first day in the garden, you mentioned a legacy received about a year ago. I have been unable to discover any trace of a legacy.
context: Poirot identifies safety as Sheppard's motive and accuses him of blackmailing Mrs. Ferrars.

text: You had to invent some way of accounting for Mrs. Ferrars's twenty thousand pounds. It has not done you much good. You lost most of it in speculation—then you put the screw on too hard, and Mrs. Ferrars took a way out that you had not expected. If Ackroyd had learnt the truth he would have had no mercy on you—you were ruined for ever.
context: Poirot explains the invented legacy, lost blackmail money, and danger of exposure.

text: I will confess to you that it was my greatest stumbling block when I found that a call had actually been put through to you from King's Abbot station. I at first believed that you had simply invented the story. It was a very clever touch, that. You must have some excuse for arriving at Fernly, finding the body, and so getting the chance to remove the dictaphone on which your alibi depended.
context: Poirot explains why the genuine telephone call initially obstructed his solution.

text: I had a very vague notion of how it was worked when I came to see your sister that first day and inquired as to what patients you had seen on Friday morning. I had no thought of Miss Russell in my mind at that time. Her visit was a lucky coincidence, since it distracted your mind from the real object of my questions. I found what I was looking for.
context: Poirot recounts how Miss Russell's visit distracted Sheppard from his inquiries about patients.

text: Among your patients that morning was the steward of an American liner. Who more suitable than he to be leaving for Liverpool by the train that evening? And afterwards he would be on the high seas, well out of the way. I noted that the _Orion_ sailed on Saturday, and having obtained the name of the steward I sent him a wireless message asking a certain question. This is his reply you saw me receive just now.
context: Poirot identifies the ship's steward as Sheppard's caller and explains the confirming wireless message.

text: The call was genuine. Your sister saw you take it. But there was only one man's word as to what was actually said—your own!
context: Poirot explains that only Sheppard's account supplied the words of the genuine call.

text: You think not? Remember what I said—the truth goes to Inspector Raglan in the morning. But, for the sake of your good sister, I am willing to give you the chance of another way out. There might be, for instance, an overdose of a sleeping draught.
context: Poirot offers Sheppard an alternative to public exposure for Caroline's sake.

text: You comprehend me? But Captain Ralph Paton must be cleared—_ça va sans dire_. I should suggest that you finish that very interesting manuscript of yours—but abandoning your former reticence.
context: Poirot requires Ralph's exoneration and a truthful conclusion to Sheppard's manuscript.

text: Now that you remind me of the fact, it is true that there is one thing more. It would be most unwise on your part to attempt to silence me as you silenced M. Ackroyd. That kind of business does not succeed against Hercule Poirot, you understand.
context: Poirot warns Sheppard that an attempt to silence him would fail.
